steps.title: Identify what the passage argues about Apichatpong's films
steps.content: The passage draws a clear distinction between sleep and boredom: Apichatpong intentionally makes films that induce sleep, and he treats this as a positive effect. Boredom, by contrast, produces anxiety. The key point is that calling his films "sleep inducing" is not self-criticism — it describes an intentional artistic outcome.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — Audiences will fail to enjoy both *Solaris* and *Uncle Boonmee Who Can Recall His Past Lives* if they confuse sleepiness with boredom.: The passage never claims audiences fail to enjoy the films; it argues for the value of the sleep effect. ✗
  • Choice B — Slow-cinema films can relieve anxiety for those who embrace their languid pace, but the films increase anxiety for those experiencing boredom as a result of the lack of a cohesive narrative.: The passage does not distinguish between audiences who embrace vs. resist the pace; this misreads the sleep/boredom contrast. ✗
  • Choice C — *Solaris* and many other slow cinema films often induce anxiety in their audiences due to their measured pace, but films by Apichatpong do not, despite possessing other traits characteristic of the genre.: The passage attributes anxiety to boredom, not to slow cinema in general, and does not carve out Apichatpong as the lone exception. ✗
  • Choice D — It would be a misunderstanding to interpret Apichatpong's reflection on his work as a confession of its failure, because the effects his films produce on audiences are intentional.: This captures the passage's core argument: the sleep effect is intentional, so describing it is not an admission of failure. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
